title: How to Hire a Designer With No Design Experience
excerpt: Hiring a designer when you have no design background can feel intimidating. How do you evaluate a portfolio? How do you know if a proposal is good value? This guide gives you a practical framework so you can hire with confidence — even without a design eye.
content:
You need a website or app designed, but you have no background in design. How do you know if someone's work is actually good? How do you avoid being talked into things you don't need? And how do you make sure you get what you're paying for?

Here's your practical guide.

Start With Results, Not Aesthetics

When reviewing a portfolio, don't just ask "do I like how this looks?" Ask:

  • Does this site/app help the business achieve its goals?

  • Is the navigation logical and easy to understand?

  • Does the design communicate the brand clearly?

  • Would I trust this business based on this website?


Results-focused thinking will serve you far better than trying to judge visual aesthetics without training.

Look for Clear Communication

A great designer doesn't just deliver visual work — they explain their thinking. When a designer can articulate why they made certain choices (not just what they chose), you're dealing with a strategic thinker, not just a pixel pusher.

During your first conversations, evaluate:

  • Do they ask questions about your business and goals?

  • Do they explain their process clearly?

  • Do they listen more than they talk?


Trust the Process, Not Just the Output

A designer with a clear, documented process is lower risk than one who wings it. Ask to see a sample project timeline or proposal. Does it include discovery, wireframes, design reviews, and a revision process? A structured approach protects your investment.

Ask for a Ballpark Comparison

If you're not sure whether a quote is reasonable, get three quotes for the same scope. This gives you calibration. The lowest isn't always the best, but significant outliers in either direction deserve scrutiny.

You Don't Need to Be a Designer to Know If Something's Wrong

Trust your instincts. If something feels confusing, cluttered, or off-brand, it probably is. You are the customer — and your business is the subject matter expert. A good designer respects your feedback and can explain why they recommend what they do.

Take the Guesswork Out of It
